aliases: 菲律宾花生炖牛尾 (Fēilǜbīn Huā Shēng Dùn Niú Wěi)
Fēilǜbīn Huā Shēng Dùn Niú Wěi

chinese: 菲律宾花生炖牛尾 (Fēilǜbīn Huā Shēng Dùn Niú Wěi)
romanized: Fēilǜbīn Huā Shēng Dùn Niú Wěi

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Kare-Kare; closest parent 'stew' used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to Kare-Kare; closest parent 'Peanut stew' (A0CCG) used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for Kare-Kare.

serving.description: 1 plate (~400g main dish without rice, with 1 tablespoon bagoong ~15g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One plate of Kare-Kare, approximately 400g, consists of oxtail and tripe in a rich peanut-annatto sauce with vegetables, served without rice but accompanied by a tablespoon of bagoong fermented shrimp paste on the side.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia — Stewed meat dish with peanut sauce (closest match); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data for oxtail, peanuts, and tripe
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source contains a direct Kare-Kare entry. Nutrient values are adapted from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for peanut-based meat stews and USDA FoodData Central component data for oxtail (USDA FDC ID 174032), beef tripe (USDA FDC ID 169451), peanuts (USDA FDC ID 172430), and mixed vegetables. The per-100g values from the input record (sourced from FNRI-DOST Philippines PFCT, an approved FAO/INFOODS-affiliated regional database) are consistent with these approved-source component estimates and are retained. FNRI-DOST PFCT is recognized as an approved FAO/INFOODS SEA regional member database.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Peanuts and mixed peanut-based dishes (analogous estimate); University of Sydney GI Database entry for peanuts and peanut-containing foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kare-Kare in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~30 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) peanuts have a published GI of 14-23 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) the dominant macronutrient in Kare-Kare sauce is peanut fat (~32g per 400g serving), which substantially suppresses postprandial glucose response; (3) mixed peanut-based stews with high fat content are consistently classified as low-GI foods in peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). GL of ~6.6 is calculated from GI 30 × available carbohydrate 22g per serving ÷ 100 = 6.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from bagoong fermented shrimp paste condiment
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit bagoong to 1 teaspoon (~5g) rather than the standard 1 tablespoon (~15g), as the main dish provides ~500mg sodium but a full tablespoon of bagoong adds 600–800mg, bringing the meal total to ~1,100–1,300mg — approximately 55–65% of the daily sodium limit for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low glycemic load (~6.6) driven by high peanut fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kare-Kare is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI (~30) and GL (~6.6); the high peanut fat content slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption, and peanut MUFA (oleic acid) has Grade B evidence for improving insulin sensitivity in T2DM.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat and cholesterol from oxtail and tripe, compounded by high sodium from bagoong
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Kare-Kare in moderation, limiting portion size of oxtail and strictly restricting bagoong to 1 teaspoon to manage both saturated fat intake (~8g per 100g) and sodium load from the condiment.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA from peanuts and connective tissue collagen from oxtail; high sodium from bagoong is a caution for patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kare-Kare may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit from peanut MUFA and glycine/proline from oxtail collagen, but patients with RA on corticosteroids should strictly limit bagoong intake due to its high sodium content (~600mg per tablespoon).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kare-Kare is a traditional Filipino festive dish of Kapampangan origin, featuring oxtail and tripe slow-braised for several hours in a thick, rich sauce made from ground roasted peanuts and annatto (achuete) seeds, which give the sauce its characteristic golden-orange colour. The dish includes vegetables such as banana blossom (puso ng saging), eggplant (talong), and long beans (sitaw), and is always served with bagoong — fermented shrimp paste — on the side, which is considered essential to the dish's flavour identity. Texturally, the sauce is dense and nutty, the oxtail is fall-off-the-bone tender from long braising, and the bagoong provides a sharp, salty, umami counterpoint. Nutritionally, a standard plate (~400g without rice) provides approximately 600 kcal, 28g protein, 32g fat, and 22g carbohydrates. The glycemic index is low (~30) and glycemic load is low (~6.6), driven by the dominant peanut fat content which slows gastric emptying. The dish itself contains ~500mg sodium, but a tablespoon of bagoong adds 600–800mg additional sodium — a critical clinical consideration for hypertensive patients. Peanut MUFA (oleic acid) has Grade B evidence for improving insulin sensitivity in Type 2 Diabetes. Kare-Kare is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is a culturally significant dish served at Filipino family celebrations and festive occasions.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data and USDA FoodData Central component data for oxtail, tripe, peanuts, and mixed vegetables. The FNRI-DOST Philippines PFCT, cited in the input record, is an approved FAO/INFOODS-affiliated regional database. GI is an analogous estimate grounded in University of Sydney GI Database data for peanuts and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care).
notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile and moisture data for Kare-Kare as a composite dish are not separately reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for plant-oil and peanut-based sauce with no industrial hydrogenated fats). GI of ~30 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database peanut data and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Kare-Kare exists in the approved GI database. Sodium values reflect the main dish only (~125mg/100g, ~500mg per 400g plate); bagoong condiment adds 600–800mg sodium per tablespoon and must be counselled separately for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients.
